title: Dog Wheezing When Exhaling: Causes, Urgency, and Care Tips
meta_desc: Find out why your dog is wheezing when they exhale, the urgency level, and how to help your pet breathe easier before your vet appointment.
content_html:
TL;DR: Wheezing in dogs is a whistling sound caused by narrowed airways that typically requires a vet visit within 24-48 hours, though blue gums or labored breathing require immediate emergency care.

What does it mean if my dog is wheezing?

  • Wheezing is a high-pitched, whistling sound that occurs when air is forced through narrowed or constricted airways.
  • When it happens specifically during exhalation, it often suggests an issue within the lower respiratory tract, such as the bronchi or lungs.
  • Common causes can range from seasonal allergies and kennel cough to more chronic conditions like canine asthma or bronchitis.

When is dog wheezing considered a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for a dog wheezing when exhaling is generally Medium.
  • If your dog is otherwise acting normally, eating, and has pink gums, they typically need an appointment within 24 to 48 hours.
  • Seek immediate emergency care if wheezing is accompanied by a blue or purple tint to the tongue, extreme lethargy, or if your dog is using their abdominal muscles to pump air.

Why should I record a video of my dog wheezing for the vet?

  • Because wheezing can be intermittent, it often stops the moment you walk into the veterinary clinic.
  • Capturing a clear video allows your veterinarian to see the effort required for breathing and hear the specific pitch of the sound.
  • This visual and auditory evidence helps differentiate between upper airway obstructions and lower airway inflammation.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Wheezing during exhalation in dogs can be indicative of several underlying conditions affecting both large and small airways. Large airway pathology includes foreign bodies, edema, laryngeal paralysis or paresis, tracheal collapse, elongated soft palate, aspiration of stomach contents, neoplasia, and pharyngeal hematomas. Small airway obstruction can result from anaphylactic reactions, asthma, or bronchial obstruction from edema, mucus, exudates, or foreign material. Severe cases manifest as labored breathing with an expiratory push of the diaphragm, cyanosis, and anxiety; auscultation reveals high-pitched wheezes throughout the lung field. Upper respiratory tract obstructions can cause marked expiratory stridor, often with the head and forelegs extended, and a thrill may be felt at or just below the larynx. Concurrent inspiratory and expiratory dyspnea can result from tracheal obstructions (neoplasia, foreign body, or stenosis) or a combination of upper and lower obstructive airway diseases. Unconscious, apneic animals require immediate tracheal intubation and relief of any obstruction.

Chapter: Emergency, Anesthesiology, Respiratory

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1662)

faqs.question: Can allergies cause my dog to wheeze when exhaling?
faqs.answer: Yes, environmental allergens like pollen, dust, or smoke can cause inflammation in the airways, leading to wheezing and coughing.

faqs.question: Is wheezing the same as reverse sneezing?
faqs.answer: No. Reverse sneezing is a series of forceful inhalations through the nose that sounds like snorting, whereas wheezing is a musical or whistling sound usually heard during the breath out.

faqs.question: How can I help my dog breathe easier at home?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog in a cool, well-ventilated area and avoid triggers like perfumes or cigarette smoke. However, you should never administer human respiratory medications without a vet's approval.
